Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd study for hours, watching videos and going through materials, but then I'd skip classes for days. I tried to keep a schedule but with 17 online courses, it just became overwhelming. I ended up studying mostly late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway. The 31 hours I put in each week were scattered all over the place rather than organized.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then suddenly I'd realize assignments were coming up and I'd cram everything in. Deadlines were basically my only real schedule. When I had extracurricular stuff going on, studying got pushed even later into the night. I wish I could say I had a regular routine, but that would be a lie.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification that something was due soon and that would jolt me into action. Other times, honestly, it was just boredom or avoiding something else I should be doing. The peer support group I was part of would sometimes remind me to check in, which helped. But genuine curiosity about the material? That was rare for me.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where logging in became automatic, mostly because I was anxious about falling behind. I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning like checking social media. The discussion posts actually helped because I'd want to see what others were saying. But that routine fell apart when things got really stressful and I started avoiding everything again. It was more habit born from anxiety than genuine engagement.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and watch things at my own pace without the pressure of being in an actual classroom. The discussion boards were surprisingly helpful too because seeing how my peers understood things helped me figure out if I was on track. Any resource with clear visuals or diagrams stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary readings and optional materials. With so many courses, I had to triage, and anything labeled 'optional' got pushed to the bottom. Some of the longer text-based resources just made my eyes glaze over. I also avoided some discussion threads that seemed too theoretical or where people were showing off. If it wasn't directly tied to an assignment, it probably got skipped.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to my grades or assignments. Anything that helped me complete something I needed to submit was valuable. I also appreciated activities that didn't take forever - quick videos or short interactive exercises felt manageable. If something took too long without clear payoff, my attention would drift and my anxiety would spike about all the other things I wasn't doing.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxury items I couldn't afford. Required stuff had this weight to it, this urgency that made me actually do it. Optional things were nice in theory, but when you're juggling 17 courses and feeling overwhelmed, they're the first to go. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping them, which just added to my stress. Required activities at least gave me the satisfaction of checking something off.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials to my laptop and studied them offline, especially PDFs and lecture slides. I'd screenshot important diagrams and review them on my phone during commutes or waiting around. I also used YouTube videos to supplement what we were learning when the course materials weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials that weren't being tracked anywhere.
Q10: I'd often have a lecture video playing while taking handwritten notes in a notebook - the LMS would show I watched it once, but I was actually deeply engaged. Or I'd download a PDF, print it out, and spend hours annotating it by hand. Study sessions with peers from my support group happened over coffee, not online. My best thinking happened while walking around reviewing concepts in my head, which obviously leaves no digital trace.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once to download it, then work with it for days offline. The lecture slides especially - I'd download them at the start of a module and reference them constantly without ever logging back in. It was more convenient and didn't require internet. Plus, working offline meant fewer distractions and less anxiety about seeing all the other things I hadn't done yet in the LMS.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. When my anxiety was really bad, even logging in felt overwhelming because I'd see all the things I was behind on. Mid-semester when multiple deadlines piled up across different courses, I just shut down for a bit. My attendance dropped because showing up felt pointless when I was already so behind. Those were the times I relied most on peer support to pull me back.
Q13: Constantly. The workload from 17 courses was genuinely crushing sometimes. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. My extracurriculars competed for time and mental energy. There were weeks where I'd only use the LMS for the absolute essentials because I simply couldn't handle more. Fatigue made everything take twice as long and feel three times harder.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other assignments or just zoned out. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials at 2 AM when I was too tired to actually process anything but too anxious to sleep. I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in. Being physically present in the LMS didn't mean I was mentally present.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. The anxiety of putting my thoughts out there for others to judge was paralyzing sometimes. I'd avoid starting big assignments because the scope felt overwhelming, even when breaking them down would have helped. Procrastination was my coping mechanism for anxiety, which is ironic because it just created more anxiety. I knew it was self-sabotage but couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire operating system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd check the LMS obsessively as deadlines approached, cramming everything into the last possible hours. The deadline pressure actually helped focus my scattered attention, even if it wasn't healthy. My LMS activity probably showed these huge spikes right before things were due, which is embarrassing but true.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities. When I got positive comments on assignments, it reduced my anxiety about participating. But waiting for feedback was torture - I'd refresh constantly, which probably inflated my login numbers. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me avoid that type of work for a while. The peer support helped me process feedback without spiraling too much.
Q18: The course structure kind of forced engagement through deadlines and required activities, which I actually needed. Having discussion posts as part of the grade meant I couldn't just lurk forever. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes when I was struggling - there wasn't much flexibility for bad mental health days. The weekly rhythm helped create some predictability in my chaotic approach to studying.
Q19: Clear deadlines and well-organized modules made engagement easier because I could see what needed to happen when. Visual materials and video content suited my learning style perfectly. But courses with too many small tasks spread across different sections made my anxiety worse - I'd lose track and panic. Long blocks of text without visual breaks were harder for me to engage with meaningfully.
Q20: That was kind of me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, reading discussions, but not actually producing anything because I was paralyzed by perfectionism or anxiety. High activity can mean someone is struggling to understand and keeps revisiting materials. Or they might be using the LMS as procrastination from the actual work. Activity doesn't equal productivity, especially when you're stressed.
Q21: That also describes me in some ways. My attendance was terrible but my assignment scores were high because I learned a lot offline and crammed effectively before deadlines. Some people are just efficient - they get what they need quickly and work independently. The LMS is a tool, not the whole learning experience. You can be deeply engaged with course material without constantly being logged in.
Q22: Logs can show patterns like when someone accesses materials and how often, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement - whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline study, mental state, or whether the time spent was meaningful. A lot of real learning is invisible to any tracking system.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time or wanting to discuss ideas with others. For me, engagement fluctuates - sometimes I'm genuinely curious and invested, other times I'm just surviving. True engagement feels different from anxiety-driven activity, even if they look similar from outside.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels or how much effort it took just to log in some days. It doesn't capture the peer support conversations that kept me going or the offline study that actually helped things click. My high assignment completion rate despite low attendance tells a story of struggling but ultimately succeeding that the raw numbers don't explain. The emotional labor of being a student with high anxiety is completely invisible in the data.
